trueFeatures: It is a solo song in an opera, oratorio or similar large vocal work.
It usually has a lyrical, memorable melody with accompaniment.
It usually expresses feelings rather than quickly moving the story forward.
MC-0144.falseFeature: It is a low double-reed woodwind with a long folded tube.

trueFeatures: It is a strophic folk song from North-East Scotland.
It is traditionally sung unaccompanied by a male voice, often in Scots dialect.
Its story often concerns farm work, local life or relationships.
MC-0145.falseFeature: It is a shallow Irish frame drum played with a double-ended beater.

trueFeatures: It fuses Celtic or Scottish folk music with rock.
It may use traditional instruments and folk melodies.
It may add electric guitar, bass guitar, drum kit and amplified vocals.
MC-0146.falseFeature: It is a joined pair of small, high-pitched hand drums.

trueFeatures: It is a large group of singers in a work such as an opera or oratorio.
The singers usually perform in several vocal parts.
The group may comment on or take part in the action.
MC-0147.falseFeature: It is a pair of handheld shell-shaped clappers.

trueFeatures: It belongs to the musical period from about 1750 to 1810.
It often uses clear melodies, balanced phrases and mostly homophonic texture.
Common forms include sonata, concerto, symphony and string quartet.
MC-0148.falseFeature: It is a small Scottish Gaelic harp plucked with the fingers.

trueFeatures: It is a psalm sung in Scottish Gaelic, traditionally in Western Isles churches.
It is unaccompanied and is often led line by line by a precentor.
The congregation follows in a highly ornamented, overlapping texture.
MC-0149.falseFeature: It tells a player to use a mute.

trueFeatures: It is religious vocal music rooted in African American church traditions.
It often uses expressive singing, call and response and hand-clapping.
It may feature a strong rhythm, improvisation and popular-music instruments.
MC-0150.falseFeature: A wind or brass player rolls the tongue or uvula while blowing.

trueFeatures: Course examples are often identified by sitar and tabla.
It often features a drone and an improvised, ornamented melody.
It often uses repeating rhythmic cycles.
MC-0151.falseFeature: It is a brass instrument made from a long coil of tubing.

trueFeatures: It is a later-20th-century style based on short repeated patterns.
The music changes gradually by adding, removing or layering notes and parts.
Phasing and additive processes are common techniques.
MC-0152.falseFeature: It is a pair of face-to-face cymbals mounted on a pedal-operated stand.

trueFeatures: It is an extended form of solo Highland bagpipe music, also called ceol mor.
It develops one theme through increasingly elaborate variations.
It uses many grace notes above continuous drones.
MC-0153.falseFeature: It is a medium female voice between soprano and alto.

trueFeatures: It is a large-scale, multi-movement work for orchestra.
Its movements contrast, and the first is often in sonata form.
A Classical example often follows a fast-slow-minuet and trio-fast plan.
MC-0154.falseFeature: Women traditionally sing it while rhythmically beating newly woven cloth.

trueFeatures: Women traditionally sing it while rhythmically beating newly woven cloth.
It is a Scottish Gaelic work song, usually unaccompanied with a steady pulse.
A leader and group often create call and response.
MC-0155.falseFeature: It is a half-sized flute that sounds one octave higher than written.

trueFeatures: It has no tonal centre or functional major or minor key.
Its pitches may be treated as equally important.
Its harmony often sounds unsettled or dissonant.
MC-0156.falseFeature: It uses tonic, subdominant and dominant triads in a major key.

trueFeatures: It uses tonic, subdominant and dominant triads in a major key.
Chords I, IV and V are major, while chord VI is minor.
These chords create familiar song patterns and the four main cadence types.
MC-0157.falseFeature: It has no tonal centre or functional major or minor key.

trueFeatures: Its notes move in semitone steps.
It may include notes from outside the main key.
Its complete scale contains all twelve pitch classes in semitone steps.
MC-0158.falseFeature: Its six notes are separated only by whole tones, with no semitones.

trueFeatures: It is a dense chord made from adjacent or very closely spaced notes.
It creates strong dissonance.
It may be played with fingers, a fist, a forearm or several instruments.
MC-0159.falseFeature: It moves from chord V to chord I.

trueFeatures: It involves at least two melodic parts.
The parts move at the same time in opposite directions.
As one part rises, the other falls, or vice versa.
MC-0160.falseFeature: It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below.

trueFeatures: It is a distinct secondary melody heard with the main melody.
It has its own recognisable melodic shape.
It complements or contrasts with the main tune while remaining less important.
MC-0161.falseFeature: It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below.

trueFeatures: It is a high vocal countermelody.
It is sung above the main tune.
It often enters in a later verse to add brightness and a fuller texture.
MC-0162.falseFeature: It is a medium-low adult male voice between tenor and bass.

trueFeatures: It is a continuous slide from one pitch to another.
It passes rapidly through the pitches in between.
It can be performed by voices, strings, trombone, harp or piano.
MC-0163.falseFeature: It rapidly alternates between a main note and the scale note above.

trueFeatures: It is a very short ornamental note immediately before a main note.
It is written smaller than an ordinary note.
It is played quickly so the main note remains the focus.
MC-0164.falseFeature: It is a closing passage added after the main structure.

trueFeatures: It is a two-chord phrase that ends on chord V.
The dominant chord creates an expectation of chord I.
It sounds open or unfinished.
MC-0165.falseFeature: It moves from chord V to chord I.

trueFeatures: It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below.
The sustained note is heard above the changing chords.
It is the high-register counterpart of a bass pedal.
MC-0166.falseFeature: It repeats a bass melody or pattern while upper parts change.

trueFeatures: It sings several notes on one syllable.
It allows decorative vocal writing.
It contrasts with syllabic word setting.
MC-0167.falseFeature: It gives one note to each syllable.

trueFeatures: It is a deliberate change from one key to another within a piece.
The new key may be temporary or remain for the rest of the music.
The new key is established through its notes, chords and cadences.
MC-0168.falseFeature: It has no tonal centre or functional major or minor key.

trueFeatures: It moves from chord V to chord I.
It uses dominant-to-tonic movement.
It gives a strong sense of completion or a full close.
MC-0169.falseFeature: It is a two-chord phrase that ends on chord V.

trueFeatures: It raises or lowers the pitch of a sounding note continuously.
The pitch moves smoothly rather than in fixed steps.
It can use a bent guitar string, pitch wheel, lip pressure or sliding voice.
MC-0170.falseFeature: Its notes move in semitone steps.

trueFeatures: It equals half a tone and is the nearest-key distance on a piano.
It is the smallest standard interval in Western tonal music.
On a fretted guitar, it is the distance from one fret to the next.
MC-0171.falseFeature: It equals two semitones, such as C to D.

trueFeatures: It gives one note to each syllable.
It helps make sung words clear.
It contrasts with melismatic word setting.
MC-0172.falseFeature: It sings several notes on one syllable.

trueFeatures: It equals two semitones, such as C to D.
It is twice the size of a semitone.
On a fretted guitar, it usually spans two frets.
MC-0173.falseFeature: It equals half a tone and is the nearest-key distance on a piano.

trueFeatures: It rapidly alternates between a main note and the scale note above.
It may be shown by tr., a wavy line or both.
It often signals the end of a concerto cadenza.
MC-0174.falseFeature: It is a continuous slide from one pitch to another.

trueFeatures: Its six notes are separated only by whole tones, with no semitones.
Every step in the scale is the same size.
It weakens the tonic and creates an ambiguous, floating quality.
MC-0175.falseFeature: Its complete scale contains all twelve pitch classes in semitone steps.

trueFeatures: It includes the time signatures 6/8, 9/8 and 12/8.
It has two, three or four dotted-crotchet beats in each bar.
Each main beat divides into three quavers.
MC-0176.falseFeature: It is a moderate tempo marking, neither fast nor slow.

trueFeatures: It places contrasting rhythmic groupings in different parts at the same time.
The parts imply different accent patterns or competing metres.
Examples include three against two and grouping 4/4 as 3+3+2.
MC-0177.falseFeature: It gradually makes the tempo slower over a short passage.

trueFeatures: It is a moderate tempo marking, neither fast nor slow.
It is similar to, but not exactly the same as, andante.
It is often about 108-120 beats per minute, depending on context.
MC-0178.falseFeature: It gradually makes the tempo slower over a short passage.

trueFeatures: It gradually makes the tempo slower over a short passage.
It is abbreviated to rit.
It has the same practical effect as rallentando.
MC-0179.falseFeature: It is a moderate tempo marking, neither fast nor slow.

trueFeatures: It gives the performer expressive flexibility with tempo.
The performer slightly speeds up and slows down while keeping the broader flow.
It is especially associated with Romantic performance and means 'robbed time'.
MC-0180.falseFeature: It is the largest and lowest standard brass instrument.

trueFeatures: It is a repeating broken-chord accompaniment.
Its broken-chord pattern is lowest-highest-middle-highest.
It is often played by the piano left hand in Classical music.
MC-0181.falseFeature: It repeats a bass melody or pattern while upper parts change.

trueFeatures: It has two contrasting sections, A followed by B.
Either or both sections may be repeated.
A common repeated layout is AABB.
MC-0182.falseFeature: Its main A section returns between contrasting episodes.

trueFeatures: It is a closing passage added after the main structure.
It confirms and rounds off the ending.
It may bring back musical material heard earlier.
MC-0183.falseFeature: It is a contrasting passage between returns of a rondo's main A section.

trueFeatures: It combines two or more independent melodic lines.
Each part keeps its own melodic interest.
The independent lines fit together harmonically.
MC-0184.falseFeature: One main melody is supported by chords or parts moving in the same rhythm.

trueFeatures: It is a contrasting passage between returns of a rondo's main A section.
It belongs between statements of the main rondo theme.
Different examples are usually labelled B, C and so on.
MC-0185.falseFeature: It is a closing passage added after the main structure.

trueFeatures: It repeats a bass melody or pattern while upper parts change.
The repeated bass provides a steady foundation.
The music above it can develop through variations.
One main melody is supported by chords or parts moving in the same rhythm.
The accompanying parts move together rather than as independent melodies.
The main melodic line is usually easy to identify.
MC-0187.falseFeature: It contains two or more independent melodic lines sounding together.

trueFeatures: It contains two or more independent melodic lines sounding together.
Each part has its own melodic shape and rhythmic identity.
It is close in meaning to contrapuntal texture.
MC-0188.falseFeature: One main melody is supported by chords or parts moving in the same rhythm.

trueFeatures: Its main A section returns between contrasting episodes.
Common patterns include ABACA and ABACADA.
The recurring A section usually returns in the same key.
MC-0189.falseFeature: It has two contrasting sections, A followed by B.

trueFeatures: Each verse is sung to the same music.
The words change from verse to verse.
It creates a repeated musical structure across several verses.
MC-0190.falseFeature: It is a North Indian plucked long-necked lute with movable frets.

trueFeatures: The bass line moves on each beat.
It outlines chords using scale steps, arpeggio notes and passing notes.
It is especially common in jazz, blues and rock 'n' roll.
MC-0191.falseFeature: It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below.

trueFeatures: Only voices perform; there is no instrumental accompaniment.
The singers may perform in unison.
The singers may perform in several harmony parts.
MC-0192.falseFeature: It tells string players to use the bow.

trueFeatures: It tells string players to use the bow.
It cancels a pizzicato instruction.
It restores the normal bowed string sound.
MC-0193.falseFeature: It tells string players to pluck instead of using the bow.

trueFeatures: It is a medium-low adult male voice between tenor and bass.
It has some of the upper flexibility of a tenor.
It also has some of the depth of a bass voice.
MC-0194.falseFeature: It is a medium female voice between soprano and alto.

trueFeatures: It is a low double-reed woodwind with a long folded tube.
It has a reedy, resonant tone.
It often provides bass or tenor lines in the woodwind section.
MC-0195.falseFeature: It is a high double-reed woodwind with a narrow conical bore.

trueFeatures: It is a shallow Irish frame drum played with a double-ended beater.
The player holds it in one hand and strikes it with the other.
The supporting hand presses the skin from behind to change pitch and tone.
MC-0196.falseFeature: It is a joined pair of small, high-pitched hand drums.

trueFeatures: It is a joined pair of small, high-pitched hand drums.
The two drums are different sizes and are played with fingers and palms.
They are common in Afro-Cuban and Latin American music.
MC-0197.falseFeature: It is a shallow Irish frame drum played with a double-ended beater.

trueFeatures: It is a pair of handheld shell-shaped clappers.
The untuned percussion pieces are clicked together.
It is strongly linked to flamenco and Spanish dance music.
MC-0198.falseFeature: It is a pair of face-to-face cymbals mounted on a pedal-operated stand.

trueFeatures: It is a small Scottish Gaelic harp plucked with the fingers.
It is used in Scottish and Celtic traditional music.
It has no pedals; levers or blades may alter individual strings.
MC-0199.falseFeature: It is a North Indian plucked long-necked lute with movable frets.

trueFeatures: It tells a player to use a mute.
The mute may make the tone softer, thinner, veiled or more distant.
Senza sordino cancels the instruction.
MC-0200.falseFeature: It tells string players to pluck instead of using the bow.

trueFeatures: A wind or brass player rolls the tongue or uvula while blowing.
It creates a rapid fluttering or growling effect.
The effect is produced on a sustained note.
MC-0201.falseFeature: It is a tail of dense sound reflections after the original sound.

trueFeatures: It is a brass instrument made from a long coil of tubing.
It has valves, a flared bell and a rounded, mellow tone.
It sits between the high trumpets and the low brass.
MC-0202.falseFeature: It is the largest and lowest standard brass instrument.

trueFeatures: It is a pair of face-to-face cymbals mounted on a pedal-operated stand.
The player may use sticks, the pedal, or both.
Open cymbals ring longer, while closed cymbals sound short and crisp.
MC-0203.falseFeature: It is a pair of handheld shell-shaped clappers.

trueFeatures: It is a medium female voice between soprano and alto.
It is lower and often warmer than a soprano voice.
It remains higher than an alto voice.
MC-0204.falseFeature: It is a medium-low adult male voice between tenor and bass.

trueFeatures: It is a high double-reed woodwind with a narrow conical bore.
It has a clear, penetrating and slightly nasal tone.
It often plays expressive solos and gives the orchestra its tuning note.
MC-0205.falseFeature: It is a low double-reed woodwind with a long folded tube.

trueFeatures: It is a half-sized flute that sounds one octave higher than written.
It is the smallest and highest standard orchestral woodwind.
It has a bright, piercing tone.
MC-0206.falseFeature: It is the largest and lowest standard brass instrument.

trueFeatures: It tells string players to pluck instead of using the bow.
It creates a short, clear sound that fades quickly.
Its usual abbreviation is pizz.
MC-0207.falseFeature: It tells string players to use the bow.

trueFeatures: It is a tail of dense sound reflections after the original sound.
It can make a recording seem to be in a larger, more resonant space.
Its reflections merge together rather than forming one separate echo.
MC-0208.falseFeature: Very rapid percussion strokes merge into a sustained or trembling sound.

trueFeatures: Very rapid percussion strokes merge into a sustained or trembling sound.
They are common on snare drum and timpani.
They may use single-stroke, double-stroke or tremolo patterns.
MC-0209.falseFeature: It is a tail of dense sound reflections after the original sound.

trueFeatures: It is a North Indian plucked long-necked lute with movable frets.
It has melody strings and sympathetic strings.
Its resonant, buzzing tone often identifies Indian music.
MC-0210.falseFeature: It is a small Scottish Gaelic harp plucked with the fingers.

trueFeatures: It is a pair of tuned hand drums used in North Indian music.
The two drums differ in size and pitch.
They produce many contrasting strokes and rhythmic patterns.
MC-0211.falseFeature: It is a joined pair of small, high-pitched hand drums.

trueFeatures: It is the largest and lowest standard brass instrument.
It provides the bass foundation of an orchestra.
It also supplies bass notes in brass bands and wind bands.
MC-0212.falseFeature: It is the smallest and highest standard orchestral woodwind.

trueFeatures: It is a bowed string instrument slightly larger than a violin.
It sounds lower than a violin and has a darker, warmer tone.
It often plays inner harmony or melodic lines.
MC-0213.falseFeature: It is a brass instrument made from a long coil of tubing.

trueFeatures: It is written for a small ensemble, usually with one player on each part.
It is usually performed without a conductor.
Its independent parts interact closely, as in a string quartet or piano trio.
MC-0214.falseFeature: It is a major or minor triad with a sixth above the root added.

trueFeatures: It is a late-19th- and early-20th-century style focused on atmosphere and tone colour.
It may use whole-tone or modal scales, unresolved harmony and parallel chords.
Delicate orchestration, pedal and cross-rhythms may blur pulse and sonority.
MC-0215.falseFeature: It is a four-note chord built from stacked minor thirds.

trueFeatures: It fuses jazz harmony and improvisation with funk rhythm.
A strong syncopated groove is driven by drum kit and electric bass.
Electric piano, guitar, saxophone or synthesiser may play riffs and improvise.
MC-0216.falseFeature: It is a three-note chord made from two stacked minor thirds.

trueFeatures: It is a German Romantic art song for solo voice and piano.
The expressive piano part closely reflects the words, images and mood.
It may be strophic or through-composed, with voice and piano as equal partners.
MC-0217.falseFeature: It is chord V with a minor seventh added above its root.

trueFeatures: It sets the fixed sections of the Roman Catholic service to music.
Its movements include Kyrie, Gloria, Credo, Sanctus, Benedictus and Agnus Dei.
It traditionally uses Latin and may range from a cappella choir to choir and orchestra.
MC-0218.falseFeature: It raises the seventh scale degree in both ascending and descending forms.

trueFeatures: It is constructed from recorded real-world sounds.
Recordings may be cut, reversed, repeated, layered, filtered or transformed.
Playback speed may change, and sound fragments may be spliced or looped.
MC-0219.falseFeature: It moves from chord V to chord VI instead of chord I.

trueFeatures: It is a large concert work for soloists, choir and orchestra, usually telling a sacred story.
It often includes recitatives, arias, ensembles and choruses.
It is performed without theatrical acting, scenery or costumes.
MC-0220.falseFeature: It is the distance in pitch between two notes.

trueFeatures: It is early monophonic sacred vocal music, usually sung in Latin.
It uses one unaccompanied unison melody with modal pitch organisation.
Its flexible rhythm follows the text and has no regular bar-based metre.
MC-0221.falseFeature: It raises the sixth and seventh degrees when ascending.

trueFeatures: It is speech-like solo singing whose rhythm follows the words.
It delivers dialogue and moves the story forward in opera or oratorio.
It usually has flexible rhythm and sparse accompaniment and may lead into an aria.
MC-0222.falseFeature: It uses a mode rather than a familiar major or minor scale.

trueFeatures: It is a multi-movement work for solo instrument, or one instrument with piano.
It often has three or four contrasting movements.
Its first movement often uses sonata form; a common three-movement plan is fast-slow-fast.
MC-0223.falseFeature: It is a short ornament that moves to a neighbouring note and returns.

trueFeatures: It is an African American style developed from gospel, rhythm and blues and blues.
It features expressive vocals, call and response, strong groove and syncopation.
It often includes improvised decoration and a horn section.
MC-0224.falseFeature: It is the major key that shares a key signature with a minor key.

trueFeatures: It is an ensemble of two violins, viola and cello.
Each instrument is usually played by one performer.
The four players have independent parts that interact closely.
MC-0225.falseFeature: It is the major key that shares a key signature with a minor key.

trueFeatures: It is a very short crushed grace note immediately before the main note.
It is written as a small note with a slash through its stem.
It takes almost no measured time and may be played just before or on the beat.
MC-0226.falseFeature: It is written for a small ensemble, usually with one player on each part.

trueFeatures: It is a major or minor triad with a sixth above the root added.
Its extra note gives a warm, mellow colour linked to jazz and popular harmony.
Adding A to C-E-G creates C6.
MC-0227.falseFeature: It is a German Romantic art song for solo voice and piano.

trueFeatures: It is a four-note chord built from stacked minor thirds.
Its symmetrical construction creates strong tension and tonal ambiguity.
It can resolve in several directions and helps modulation to distant keys.
MC-0228.falseFeature: It sets the fixed sections of the Roman Catholic service to music.

trueFeatures: It is a three-note chord made from two stacked minor thirds.
It contains a root, minor third and diminished fifth.
Its tense root-to-fifth tritone usually seeks resolution.
MC-0229.falseFeature: It is constructed from recorded real-world sounds.

trueFeatures: It is chord V with a minor seventh added above its root.
It contains a tritone and usually resolves towards chord I.
In C major its notes are G-B-D-F, labelled V7 or G7.
MC-0230.falseFeature: It is a large concert work for soloists, choir and orchestra, usually telling a sacred story.

trueFeatures: It raises the seventh scale degree in both ascending and descending forms.
The raised leading note strengthens the pull towards the tonic.
It creates an augmented second between the sixth and seventh degrees.
MC-0231.falseFeature: It is early monophonic sacred vocal music, usually sung in Latin.

trueFeatures: It moves from chord V to chord VI instead of chord I.
It interrupts the expected ending and creates surprise.
It allows the musical phrase to continue.
MC-0232.falseFeature: It is speech-like solo singing whose rhythm follows the words.

trueFeatures: It is the distance in pitch between two notes.
Its number is found by counting letter names inclusively.
Its quality may be major, minor, perfect, augmented or diminished.
MC-0233.falseFeature: It is a multi-movement work for solo instrument, or one instrument with piano.

trueFeatures: It raises the sixth and seventh degrees when ascending.
It uses the natural-minor form when descending.
The raised notes smooth the ascent and create a strong leading note.
MC-0234.falseFeature: It is an African American style developed from gospel, rhythm and blues and blues.

trueFeatures: It uses a mode rather than a familiar major or minor scale.
Each mode has its own pattern of tones and semitones and a distinctive colour.
Dorian can use the white notes from D to D and is common in jazz improvisation.
MC-0235.falseFeature: It is an ensemble of two violins, viola and cello.

trueFeatures: It is a short ornament that moves to a neighbouring note and returns.
The neighbouring note may be above or below the main note.
Upper and lower versions use different symbols and directions.
MC-0236.falseFeature: It is the small group of featured soloists in a concerto grosso.

trueFeatures: It is a prominent decorative instrumental line accompanying a main melody.
It often interacts closely with a singer or main melodic part.
It is an essential written part, not optional decoration.
MC-0237.falseFeature: It is a very short crushed grace note immediately before the main note.

trueFeatures: It moves from chord IV to chord I.
It sounds finished but usually softer than a perfect cadence.
It is traditionally linked to the final 'Amen' of a hymn.
MC-0238.falseFeature: One part divides a span into two equal notes while another divides it into three.

trueFeatures: It is the major key that shares a key signature with a minor key.
Its tonic is three semitones above the relative minor tonic.
C major is the relative major of A minor.
MC-0239.falseFeature: It lengthens every note value of an idea by the same proportion.

trueFeatures: It is the minor key that shares a key signature with a major key.
Its tonic is three semitones below the relative major tonic.
A minor is the relative minor of C major.
MC-0240.falseFeature: It shortens every note value of an idea by the same proportion.

trueFeatures: It is a major tonic chord at the end of a piece or section in a minor key.
It raises the third of the final tonic chord.
It changes the final tonic from minor to major for a brighter close.
MC-0241.falseFeature: It uses metres such as 5/4 or 7/8 with uneven beat groupings.

trueFeatures: One part divides a span into two equal notes while another divides it into three.
Both groupings begin and end together but place their inner attacks differently.
The relationship can use note values other than quavers and triplets.
MC-0242.falseFeature: A bass instrument plays a written line while a chordal instrument realises the harmony.

trueFeatures: It lengthens every note value of an idea by the same proportion.
The transformed material takes longer but keeps its pitch and rhythm relationships.
The underlying pulse remains unchanged while the idea sounds slower.
MC-0243.falseFeature: It contrasts a small solo group with a larger ensemble in a Baroque concerto.

trueFeatures: It shortens every note value of an idea by the same proportion.
The transformed material moves faster but keeps its essential pattern.
The underlying pulse remains unchanged while the idea sounds quicker.
MC-0244.falseFeature: It is a Baroque aria in ABA form that returns to the beginning.

trueFeatures: It uses metres such as 5/4 or 7/8 with uneven beat groupings.
Its asymmetrical pulse may group beats as 2+2+3.
A 5/4 bar often groups its beats as 3+2 or 2+3.
MC-0245.falseFeature: It is an opening section that introduces the main musical ideas.

trueFeatures: The time signature changes within the music.
The change alters the number or grouping of beats in each bar.
It may create contrast, follow the words or produce rhythmic instability.
MC-0246.falseFeature: It is a variation form built over a recurring bass line and harmonic pattern.

trueFeatures: Three equal notes fit into the time usually taken by two of the same value.
A bracket and the number 3 usually mark the group.
Three triplet quavers fit into one crotchet beat.
MC-0247.falseFeature: A recurring orchestral passage alternates with contrasting solo episodes.

trueFeatures: A bass instrument plays a written line while a chordal instrument realises the harmony.
The instruments often include cello or bassoon with harpsichord, organ or lute.
It gives continuous Baroque support, with the chord voicing often improvised.
MC-0248.falseFeature: It is agile vocal writing with rapid scales, leaps, trills and other ornaments.

trueFeatures: It contrasts a small solo group with a larger ensemble in a Baroque concerto.
The concertino alternates with the ripieno to change texture and weight.
Its defining forces include concertino, ripieno and basso continuo.
MC-0249.falseFeature: It produces high, light string tones by touching a nodal point.

trueFeatures: It is a Baroque aria in ABA form that returns to the beginning.
The words da capo direct the singer back after the contrasting B section.
The returning A section is often not written out and is usually ornamented.
MC-0250.falseFeature: It is the larger body of players in a concerto grosso.

trueFeatures: It is an opening section that introduces the main musical ideas.
In sonata form it presents two subjects and their key contrast.
In a fugue it introduces the subject or answer in every voice.
MC-0251.falseFeature: It rapidly repeats or alternates notes to create a trembling sound.

trueFeatures: It is a variation form built over a recurring bass line and harmonic pattern.
The upper parts change or become more elaborate.
The repeating foundation continues beneath the variations.
MC-0252.falseFeature: It is written for a small ensemble, usually with one player on each part.

trueFeatures: A recurring orchestral passage alternates with contrasting solo episodes.
The recurring passage may return in different keys or in a shorter form.
In a concerto grosso, the ripieno often presents it between concertino episodes.
MC-0253.falseFeature: It is a late-19th- and early-20th-century style focused on atmosphere and tone colour.

trueFeatures: Its three main stages are exposition, development and recapitulation.
The development transforms ideas before both subjects return in the tonic.
A coda may follow, and the form often structures a first movement.
MC-0254.falseFeature: It fuses jazz harmony and improvisation with funk rhythm.

trueFeatures: It is the main musical theme on which a work or section is based.
It is the main idea developed through the music.
The term is used for fugue themes and first or second themes in sonata form.
MC-0255.falseFeature: It is a German Romantic art song for solo voice and piano.

trueFeatures: It uses mostly new music from beginning to end.
It has no regular return of the same music for every verse.
It suits a narrative because the music can keep changing with the words.
MC-0256.falseFeature: It sets the fixed sections of the Roman Catholic service to music.

trueFeatures: It is agile vocal writing with rapid scales, leaps, trills and other ornaments.
It is especially linked to high soprano roles and displays vocal control.
It became prominent in Baroque music and may include written or improvised decoration.
MC-0257.falseFeature: It uses metres such as 5/4 or 7/8 with uneven beat groupings.

trueFeatures: It is the small group of featured soloists in a concerto grosso.
It has a lighter texture than the full ensemble.
It alternates and contrasts with the ripieno.
MC-0258.falseFeature: A bass instrument plays a written line while a chordal instrument realises the harmony.

trueFeatures: It produces high, light string tones by touching a nodal point.
The light touch suppresses the fundamental so an upper partial sounds.
The result may sound eerie on bowed strings or bell-like on guitar and harp.
MC-0259.falseFeature: It is agile vocal writing with rapid scales, leaps, trills and other ornaments.

trueFeatures: It is the larger body of players in a concerto grosso.
It contrasts with the smaller concertino group.
Its passages create a fuller and more powerful ensemble sound.
MC-0260.falseFeature: It is the small group of featured soloists in a concerto grosso.

trueFeatures: It rapidly repeats or alternates notes to create a trembling sound.
Bowed strings often produce it with rapid bow strokes on one note.
Other instruments may repeat one note or alternate quickly between two.
MC-0261.falseFeature: It produces high, light string tones by touching a nodal point.
